items.correct: Rapid alternation between the written note and the note a step above.
items.why: Students confuse trill (rapid alternation between two notes) with vibrato (pitch oscillation around one note). Trills add ornamentation; vibrato adds expressive width to a sustained pitch.

items.q: How does a harmonic minor scale differ from a natural minor?
items.correct: The seventh degree is raised by a semitone (creating a leading note).
items.misconception: All notes are raised by a semitone.
items.why: Students think the whole scale changes. Only the 7TH is altered to create a leading note, which strengthens cadences in minor keys - producing the characteristic augmented second between 6th and 7th.

items.correct: A cadence ending on chord V, creating a sense of incompleteness.
items.misconception: A cadence that sounds wrong or is performed badly.
items.random_wrong: A cadence using only major chords.
items.why: Students think 'imperfect' means flawed. It's a technical term for an unfinished-sounding cadence - any chord moving to V leaves the music suspended, awaiting resolution.

items.correct: Rapid alternation between two adjacent notes
items.why: Correct answer: Rapid alternation between two adjacent notes. “A slow ornament” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A turn circles the main note in which pattern?
items.why: It's Above-main-below-main. “Below-main-above-main” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A four-note turn contains how many notes?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
